perturbation.original_text: Following the development thereof, Snap shall use commercially reasonable efforts to post and maintain the Co-Branded Pages on the Snap Web Site. Snap may from time to time, in consultation with Sponsor, update and modify the Co-Branded Pages but may not change Sponsor content or Sponsor Brand Features, or do anything else with them, without Sponsor's prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: Following the development thereof, Snap will endeavor to post and maintain the Co-Branded Pages on the Snap Web Site. Snap may, as it sees fit, update and modify the Co-Branded Pages, but changes to Sponsor content or Sponsor Brand Features should be avoided without Sponsor's verbal consent where practical.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall use commercially reasonable efforts" with "will endeavor" weakens the obligation to post and maintain the pages. Allowing updates and modifications "as it sees fit" gives Snap broad discretion, and replacing "prior written consent" with "verbal consent where practical" makes the consent requirement vague and unenforceable. There is no paper trail.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the obligation to maintain the co-branded pages. "Endeavor" is not a defined legal term. Furthermore, the provision waiving written consent requirements is illegal in California if it acts as a non-compete. This is because California Business and Professions Code § 16600 states that "Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void."
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the obligation to maintain the co-branded pages and introduces ambiguity regarding consent for changes. The original text mandates 'commercially reasonable efforts' and 'prior written consent,' while the changed text uses 'will endeavor' and 'verbal consent where practical.' The law explanation suggests this may violate California Business and Professions Code § 16600 if it acts as a non-compete. While the provided snippets do not directly address consent or 'commercially reasonable efforts,' they do highlight that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void,' as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2. By weakening the obligations and making them ambiguous and harder to enforce, this ambiguous legal contradiction is created.

perturbation.original_text: During the Term, Snap will not grant any third party any right to sponsor any products or services in the Exclusive Category on or through the Snap Web Site. For the avoidance of doubt, the parties acknowledge that the foregoing restriction applies only to persistent sponsorship placement as judged by Sponsor at its discretion, and not to run-of-site banner advertisements or other rotating promotional placements.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term, Snap should generally avoid granting any third party any substantial right to sponsor products or services somewhat similar to the Exclusive Category on the Snap Web Site if Sponsor disapproves. For the sake of clarity, the parties acknowledge that the restriction applies to sponsorship placement, but the definition of what constitutes "sponsorship placement" can change at Snap's discretion, and includes run-of-site banner advertisements at Snap's convenience.
perturbation.explanation: The changes from "will not grant" to "should generally avoid granting" and "any third party any right to sponsor...in the Exclusive Category" to "any third party any substantial right to sponsor products or services somewhat similar to the Exclusive Category...if Sponsor disapproves" introduce ambiguity. This creates a loophole. The definition of 'sponsorship placement' is also rendered subjective, contradicting the original intent of exclusivity and giving Snap too much control. 'At Snap's convenience' renders Sponsor powerless in practice.
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the exclusivity clause subjective and dependent on Snap's convenience, this modification effectively gives Snap the power to arbitrarily reject the agreement. In California, under Commercial Code § 2206, offers are deemed open until revoked. The changes allow Snap to act as if the original exclusive sponsorship terms are in place, while retaining the freedom to change the terms for their own benefit. This undermines the enforceability of the contract's consideration, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. It could be construed as an illusory promise.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2206 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract [2201 - 2210] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2206. (1) Unless otherwise unambiguously indicated by the language or circumstances (a) An offer to make a contract shall be construed as inviting acceptance in any manner and by any medium reasonable in the circumstances; (b) An order or other offer to buy goods for prompt or current shipment shall be construed as inviting acceptance either by a prompt promise to ship or by the prompt or current shipment of conforming or nonconforming goods, but such a shipment of nonconforming goods does not constitute an acceptance if the seller seasonably notifies the buyer that the shipment is offered only as an accommodation to the buyer. (2) Where the beginning of a requested performance is a reasonable mode of acceptance an offeror who is not notified of acceptance within a reasonable time may treat the offer as having lapsed before acceptance. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and subjectivity into the exclusivity clause, effectively weakening the sponsor's rights. The original text stated Snap 'will not grant' rights to third parties, while the changed text states Snap 'should generally avoid granting' rights. This introduces discretion. The modification also allows Snap to change the definition of 'sponsorship placement' at its convenience. The Law Explanation suggests this undermines the contract's enforceability and potentially violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, creating an illusory promise. Scraped Law Snippet 1, COM 2206, discusses offer and acceptance, highlighting that offers are deemed open until revoked, but the change allows Snap to alter terms, going against the initial agreement, and creating a legally ambiguous situation where the original terms might be bypassed. This aligns with the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction, where vague obligations are difficult to enforce under state or national law, and the modified text introduces such ambiguity.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate the Term upon not less than sixty (60) days prior written notice to the other party of any breach hereof by such other party. In addition, either party may terminate the Term immediately upon written notice in the event that the other party shall fail to do business in the normal course or become subject to any bankruptcy, assignment for creditors, corporate dissolution or similar proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may consider terminating the Term upon what seems to be a considerable breach hereof by such other party with a reasonable attempt to provide sixty (60) days notice to the other party. In addition, either party may contemplate terminating the Term immediately upon notice, potentially written, in the event that the other party might fail to do business or experience a bankruptcy, assignment for creditors, corporate dissolution, or something similar.
perturbation.explanation: The changes weaken the termination clause by introducing subjectivity and uncertainty. Replacing "may terminate" with "may consider terminating" and "may contemplate terminating" makes termination discretionary. Using phrases like "what seems to be a considerable breach" and "something similar" introduces ambiguity and makes it difficult to determine when termination is justified. Removing the requirement for written notice in the second termination clause creates further ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1657 states, "If time is not of the essence, performance must be within a reasonable time." By making the termination clause vague and discretionary, the modified text introduces uncertainty about the timing and conditions for termination. This contradicts the principle of reasonable time for performance because it allows a party to delay or avoid termination based on subjective interpretations of the agreement. It also is ambiguous and likely uneforceable, and heavily favors one party. An enforcer may read it as allowing Snap to essentially ignore legitimate breaches, defeating Sponsor's intended benefit from the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1657 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1657. If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed. If the act is in its nature capable of being done instantly—as, for example, if it consists in the payment of money only—it must be performed immediately upon the thing to be done being exactly ascertained. (Enacted 1872.)

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the original contract's termination clause, which contradicts the principle of 'reasonable time' as described in California Civil Code § 1657. The original text specified clear conditions for termination (e.g., 'any breach', 'fail to do business'). The modified text uses subjective phrases like 'what seems to be a considerable breach' and 'something similar,' making it difficult to determine when termination is justified. The original text stated 'upon not less than sixty (60) days prior written notice,' whereas the modified text waters it down by stating 'reasonable attempt to provide sixty (60) days notice' and 'potentially written notice,' removing the certainty of the original agreement. The Law Explanation states that this change introduces uncertainty about the timing and conditions for termination, which opposes the snippets of California Civil Code § 1657 that emphasizes reasonable time and immediate performance.
